In the US, clinicians tend to avoid this method because it causes the loss of protein S, antitrypsin, and antiplasmin during the removal of the solvent-detergent, leading to some fatalities. Nevertheless, certain regions of Europe still utilize this technique. retrieved_docs.title: Managing Fresh-Frozen Plasma Transfusion Adverse Effects: Allergic Reactions, TACO, and TRALI -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Viruses and bacteria can cause transfusion-transmitted infections. Notable transfusions-associated pathogens include human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), hepatitis B, and hepatitis C. These infections can be transmitted through contaminated blood products, emphasizing the importance of rigorous screening and safety measures in blood transfusion practices. Cytomegalovirus and human T-cell lymphotropic virus are typically not transmitted through fresh-frozen plasma because fresh-frozen plasma is an acellular blood component. Safety measures include retesting of plasma, known as donor-retested plasma, where units are quarantined until a follow-up negative infectious disease test from the donor, and pathogen-reduced plasma, treated with UV light or chemicals like methylene blue, to curb lipid-enveloped virus transmission, particularly outside the United States. Solvent/detergent (S/D) plasma is a method that eliminates the risk of transmission of enveloped viruses in blood products, further bolstering the safety of plasma transfusions. These techniques are crucial for reducing the potential transmission of infectious agents in blood components. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate the etiology, epidemiology, and pathophysiology of the various types of viral hepatitis. Implement evidence-based treatment and preventive strategies for viral hepatitis. Identify common complications of viral hepatitis. Apply effective interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance and improve outcomes for patients with viral hepatitis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemophilia -- Complications -- Blood-borne Infection-related Complications
retrieved_docs.content: In the 1980s, factor concentrates got contaminated with viruses like HIV and HCV, and patients who received those got infected with HIV and hepatitis C. This resulted in high mortality rates in patients with hemophilia in the 1980s and early 1990s. Today, many studies show that HIV and HCV transmission through factor concentrate has been almost eliminated due to careful selection of donors, screening techniques, viral elimination process during manufacturing, and advancement in diagnostic procedures to detect these viruses early. Higher usage of recombinant factors has dramatically decreased the risk of infection. However, new challenges from non-lipid enveloped viruses and prion diseases are emerging. Also, currently available anti-HIV medications are all safe for use in patients, with no contraindications. For patients with hepatitis C and hemophilia, pegylated interferon and ribavirin are the treatment. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemophilia -- Treatment / Management -- Plasma-derived Versus Recombinant Factor VIII
retrieved_docs.content: allowing them to get home infusion therapy. However, in the 1980s, many patients with hemophilia were affected by contaminated factor concentrates, and 60% to 70% of patients got infected with HIV. Almost 100% of patients got infected with hepatitis C. This tragedy prompted more research to make plasma-derived factor concentrate safer. Eventually, cloning of the gene for factor VIII occurred in 1984, and recombinant factor VIII concentrate became available in 1992.

retrieved_docs.title: OSHA Bloodborne Pathogen Standards -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis B is a viral infection capable of causing persistent, chronic infection that can lead to acute and chronic disease. It is a hepatotropic virus transmitted via blood or semen from infected individuals to those lacking immunity. HBV is a global health problem and a well-established occupational risk for health care workers.  The incidence of HBV in the population is steadily rising, and the prevalence of HBV infection in health care workers is much greater than in the general population. [4] The virus is structurally stable, remaining viable and infectious for prolonged periods of up to 1 week on surfaces. [5] HBV is highly infectious and efficiently transmitted through percutaneous or mucosal exposure to infectious blood or body fluids. The risk of percutaneous HBV infection varies from 6 to 30%, depending on the serology of the source. [6] [7] Vaccines to prevent HBV became available in 1981 and remained the mainstay of hepatitis B prevention. Among the safety measures listed, the Bloodborne Pathogens Standard mandates that employers ensure this vaccine's availability to all employees at risk for occupational exposure. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 285
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is classified as a member of the group of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, indicating its role within this therapeutic class. In addition to its use against HCV, ribavirin may also help prevent viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Remdesivir, another antiviral agent, may be employed to treat viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Together, ribavirin and remdesivir exemplify antiviral medications that are utilized both for treating specific viral diseases such as HCV and for broader management of viral infections, underscoring their importance in antiviral therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections and Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are both subclasses of direct acting antivirals. Within the HCV‑specific antiviral subclass, the drugs ribavirin and telaprevir are members, indicating that they are used as direct‑acting agents against hepatitis C virus infection. Rimantadin, on the other hand, is a member of the Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals subclass, and its pharmacologic action includes the physiologic effect of altering cellular synthetic activity. This organization of antiviral agents highlights how specific drugs such as ribavirin, telaprevir, and rimantadin fit into broader mechanistic classes and clarifies the cellular effect associated with rimantadin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 369
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, making it a member of the therapeutic group “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as a type of direct‑acting antivirals, and the drug ribavirin is an example of an antiviral used specifically for HCV that belongs to this class. Cyclic amines that function as direct‑acting antivirals are also considered members of the broader direct‑acting antiviral category, and the medication rimantadine (listed as “rimotadin”) is a member of this cyclic‑amine direct‑acting antiviral subclass. In addition to its antiviral activity, rimantadine produces the physiologic effect of altering cellular synthetic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct acting antivirals, a category that also includes cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals. Within the HCV‑specific antiviral class, telaprevir is identified as a member, representing a targeted therapy for hepatitis C virus infection. Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals comprise compounds such as rimantadine, which is recognized as a member of this subclass. Rimantadine exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity, reflecting its impact on host cell metabolism. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific antiviral agents are organized within broader mechanistic classes and how individual drugs can produce distinct cellular effects relevant to their therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 638
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, and telaprevir is a member of this antiviral group used specifically to treat hepatitis C virus infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are direct acting antivirals, indicating that these medications specifically target viral proteins essential for hepatitis C virus replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections” comprises agents such as ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as antiviral medications specifically to manage hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: Telaprevir is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of drugs used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 280
retrieved_docs.content: Direct acting antivirals are classified as a subset of antivirals for systemic use, indicating that these agents are administered systemically and act directly on viral targets. This relationship places direct acting antivirals within the broader category of systemic antiviral therapies, helping clinicians recognize that they belong to the larger group of antivirals intended for systemic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, as are cyclic amines with antiviral activity and nucleosides and nucleotides that do not act as reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors. Within these classes, ribavirin is a member of the HCV‑direct‑acting antiviral group, while cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, and remdesivir belong to the nucleoside/nucleotide subclass that excludes reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors. Brivudine is chemically related to bromodeoxyuridine. Rimantadine, which is a cyclic‑amine direct‑acting antiviral, exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Therapeutically, remdesivir may be used to treat viral infections, and ribavirin may help prevent viral infections. Both brincidofovir and tecovirimat have activity against smallpox, a disease that is itself a type of viral infection. This network of relationships highlights how various direct‑acting antiviral agents are grouped by chemical structure and mechanism, and how specific agents are linked to particular viral diseases, informing both drug classification and clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 830
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are classified as direct acting antivirals, indicating that this group of cyclic amine compounds belongs to the broader class of antiviral agents that directly inhibit viral replication by targeting specific viral enzymes or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 35
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE encompass a broad category of antiviral agents, within which direct acting antivirals represent a specific subclass. Direct acting antivirals include both the class of cyclic amine compounds and combination regimens used for HIV treatment. The combination category “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” is itself a direct acting antiviral and comprises specific fixed‑dose regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine. The nucleoside analogue abacavir, which is chemically identified as dideoxyadenosine, is a component of both HIV combination regimens and is therefore mapped to dideoxyadenosine. Rimantadine, a member of the cyclic amine direct acting antiviral class, exerts a physiologic effect described as alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Together, these relationships illustrate how systemic antiviral therapy is organized into mechanistic subclasses, how specific chemical entities like abacavir relate to their nucleoside analogues, and how agents such as rimantadine produce defined cellular effects, informing both pharmacologic classification and therapeutic application.
